top_findings.finding: Participant expressed frustration that they could not see an image and noted difficulty understanding content due to apparent screen reader output errors.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant stated 'glad I can't see the image' and the transcript shows garbled or severely distorted screen reader output throughout (e.g., 'brain canal' instead of 'birth canal', 'ves in urinal' instead of 'veins in anal', 'droppings' rendered inconsistently, heading levels and bullet structure misread).
top_findings.stated_signal: glad I can't see the image. No, skin tags not the same as pile... So far the information has been really well written, I think, in terms of easy to understand, comprehensive, but not too overwhelming.
top_findings.rationale: The participant uses assistive technology and the screen reader output is severely corrupted, rendering critical medical terminology unintelligible (e.g., 'brain canal' for 'birth canal', 'ves in urinal' for 'veins in anal canal'). Although the participant also praised the overall writing quality, the fundamental accessibility failure with AT prevents reliable comprehension of the health information. The stated conflict (praise + frustration) is resolved to the lower observed behaviour: the AT malfunction is a significant barrier that was worked around but not resolved.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Blind or low-vision users relying on screen readers experience structurally amplified friction due to severe OCR or text parsing errors that render medical terminology completely inaccurate. This is not a minor accessibility slip but a critical failure of semantic content delivery via AT.

top_findings.finding: Participant using a screen reader cannot access video content on the page and can only read transcripts, finding this inaccessible and ironic given the mental health messaging.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant navigated the page, encountered video listings, attempted to play videos with screen reader, but could only access transcripts instead.
top_findings.stated_signal: I can't seem to play the videos that are listed here, just read transcripts. Um, maybe that's all that's there, I don't know, but it seems like these should be videos that we can quickly watch, um, but I can't seem to work out how to watch with my screen reader... it's really quite frustrating and ironic given that this... this is a common thing across the whole champion.
top_findings.rationale: The participant using assistive technology (screen reader) cannot access video functionality; only transcripts are available to them. The stated frustration and irony recognition indicate significant barrier, and the observation that this is a recurring pattern across the site suggests a major accessibility component failure being worked around rather than fully blocked.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structural exclusion from video content that sighted users can access. The absence of accessible video controls or alternative playback methods for AT users is a systematic accessibility gap.

top_findings.finding: Participant could not figure out how to play videos on the page using a screen reader, and expressed frustration that this accessibility barrier was ironic given the content about inclusive workplaces.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant attempted to interact with video content but could not determine how to activate playback with their screen reader; they moved on without successfully playing any videos.
top_findings.stated_signal: I can't seem to work out how to watch with my screen reader. Working together the champion inclusive workplaces heading to, um, which is really quite frustrating and ironic given that this, well, and this is a common thing across the whole champion. Be able to watch videos in other parts of it as well.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated inability to interact with video controls via screen reader and expressed frustration. The observed behaviour shows they could not access the video playback functionality, and this barrier was skipped rather than resolved. This represents a significant accessibility failure on content that was intended to be consumable, fitting S3 (significant component failure) and L3 (significant friction with frustration).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structural amplification when video player controls lack proper semantic labels, focus management, or keyboard accessibility. The participant's inability to discover or activate playback is a direct result of missing or inaccessible accessibility markup on interactive video elements.

top_findings.finding: Participant encountered a table with many blank cells and expressed confusion about why the cells were empty, unable to extract meaningful plan comparison data.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant navigated through a 29-row, 3-column table using a screen reader, cycling through cells and repeatedly encountering blank rows and columns (rows 2-14, column 3 repeatedly reported as blank or 'light').
top_findings.stated_signal: I don't know why these are all blank.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about the blank cells. The observed signal shows repetitive navigation through empty content, indicating the table structure or data was not presented in a way the participant could understand or use for comparison. This constitutes significant component failure (inability to extract comparison data from the intended table) that was worked around rather than resolved.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally more vulnerable to table markup and data integrity issues. Blank cells that might be visually obvious as empty are vocalized repeatedly, increasing cognitive load and confusion when navigating large tables.

top_findings.finding: Screen reader is not correctly announcing table cell contents, particularly blank cells and column headers, causing the participant to receive incomplete information about product features.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant navigates through a table using a screen reader and repeatedly encounters blank announcements or missing content (e.g., 'column 3 blank rotary column 3 just blank blank'). The participant explicitly states expectations (e.g., 'I would expect to see two ticks there') that are not being announced.
top_findings.stated_signal: I would expect to see two ticks there, um, and a tick... the screen read is not picking up... I'd expect to see a cross there or hear it, hear it, you know, but the screen read is not picking up like column 3 blank rotary column 3 just blank blank.
top_findings.rationale: The participant is using a screen reader to access a comparison table and the accessibility layer is failing to announce critical table cell data (ticks, crosses, content). The participant's stated expectation conflicts with what the screen reader is delivering. This is a significant component failure that blocks full understanding of the comparison table, though the participant continues attempting to work around it.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally dependent on correct table semantics and cell announcements. Blank or missing announcements prevent them from accessing information sighted users can see, creating a complete accessibility barrier to this content.

coaching_recommendations.title: Review high-friction moments before lower-level issues
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 61 finding-level issue(s), with the highest severity at S3.
advice: Identify the common pattern behind the higher-severity findings.
Focus coaching on the most disruptive parts of the session first.
Avoid treating all findings as equal when severity levels differ.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S3=7, S4=12, S5=37, S6=5. Representative findings: Participant expressed frustration that they could not see an image and noted difficulty understanding content due to apparent screen reader output errors. | Participant using a screen reader cannot access video content on the page and can only read transcripts, finding this inaccessible and ironic given the mental health messaging. | Participant could not figure out how to play videos on the page using a screen reader, and expressed frustration that this accessibility barrier was ironic given the content about inclusive workplaces.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 7 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 61 findings spanning 7 distinct friction types (F2,F6,F1,F3,F5,F4,F7). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F2 (Confidence) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F6 Not Found, F1 Comprehension) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=61 findings across 7 distinct friction types (F2=20, F6=12, F1=11, F3=9, F5=7, F4=1, F7=1).

coaching_recommendations.title: Recording quality is usable but should be improved
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session is still usable for analysis, but defects in the recording may reduce confidence in detailed interpretation.
advice: Check microphone clarity and ambient noise before starting future sessions.
Reduce clipping, brief audio gaps, or interruptions where possible.
Mark this session as usable with caution if fine-grained analysis is required.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-B marks recording quality as acceptable rather than good.
